Sam Roesch
Plus

You said:

So Claude code might kill SaaS as we know it. SaaS isn’t flexible enough. I can built custom apps to suit business needs. So what will become business models going forwards for selling tech

ChatGPT said:

“SaaS is dead” is a bit like saying spreadsheets killed software engineers. It sounds right for about five minutes, and then reality shows up.

What is true: tools like Claude Code dramatically lower the cost of building custom software. That shifts where value sits—but it doesn’t eliminate it.

Let’s unpack what actually changes.

The big shift: from software as product → software as outcome

Historically:

  • SaaS sold features

  • Customers adapted their workflow to the tool

Now:

  • AI lets you build custom workflows cheaply

  • Customers expect software to adapt to them

So the value moves up the stack.

What probably gets commoditized (or dies)

These are the danger zones:

1. “Generic CRUD SaaS”

  • Basic CRM clones

  • Simple project trackers

  • Internal tools with no moat

If someone can recreate 80% of your product in a weekend with AI, pricing collapses.

2. UI-heavy but logic-light products

If the “secret sauce” is just:

  • forms

  • tables

  • dashboards

→ AI + frameworks will eat this quickly.

3. Seat-based pricing

Charging “$X per user per month” becomes harder when:

  • AI agents replace users

  • workflows become automated

What becomes MORE valuable

This is where it gets interesting—and where real businesses will form.

1. Systems of Record (Data Moats)

If you own the data layer, you’re still powerful.

Examples:

  • Salesforce (customer data)

  • QuickBooks (financial data)

Why they survive:

  • AI can build apps

  • but it can’t easily recreate trusted, structured, historical data

👉 For your world (tax at KPMG), this is massive:

  • client history

  • filings

  • positions taken

  • audit trails

That’s gold.

2. Vertical SaaS → “Embedded Expertise Platforms”

Generic tools weaken, but deep domain tools get stronger.

Think:

  • tax workflows

  • compliance engines

  • audit logic

Not just software—but encoded expertise.

👉 The moat becomes:

  • rules

  • judgment

  • edge cases

  • regulatory interpretation

AI helps build the UI—but the thinking is still valuable.

3. Outcome-based pricing

Instead of:

  • $50/user/month

You get:

  • $X per tax return completed

  • % of cost savings

  • $ per insight/action

This aligns with what AI enables: automation of outcomes, not tools.

4. Agent platforms / orchestration layers

People won’t want to wire up 15 AI agents themselves.

There’s value in:

  • coordination

  • reliability

  • observability

  • permissions

Think:

  • “Zapier for agents”

  • “Air traffic control for AI workflows”

5. Distribution becomes king

If building is cheap, getting users isn’t.

Moats shift to:

  • brand

  • trust

  • ecosystem

  • integration footprint

Example:

  • Microsoft bundling AI into everything

  • Shopify owning merchants

6. Compliance, security, and auditability

AI-built systems will be messy.

Enter:

  • governance layers

  • audit logs

  • explainability

  • regulatory compliance

Especially in your domain:

  • tax authorities won’t accept “the AI did it”

What new business models look like

Here are the patterns I’d bet on:

1. “AI-enabled services” (hybrid model)

Not pure SaaS.

  • AI does 80%

  • humans handle edge cases

  • priced as a service

You reduce cost structure → increase margin.

2. “Platform + customization layer”

Core platform + AI customization

  • Base system (data + workflows)

  • AI builds client-specific layers on top

Kind of like:

Salesforce + AI building your custom org instantly

3. “Internal tool builders as products”

Sell the ability to build tools, not the tools.

  • but with guardrails

  • templates

  • domain logic baked in

4. “Data network effects businesses”

The more people use it:

  • the smarter it gets

  • the harder it is to leave

Especially strong in:

  • tax

  • legal

  • healthcare

The uncomfortable truth

AI doesn’t kill SaaS.

It kills:

  • lazy SaaS

  • thin-feature SaaS

  • undifferentiated SaaS

But it amplifies:

  • deep domain expertise

  • data ownership

  • workflow integration

Bringing it back to YOU (this is the interesting part)

You’re sitting in a very advantaged position.

You already have:

  • proprietary workflows (tax)

  • real user problems

  • access to data

  • distribution inside KPMG

The opportunity isn’t:

“build SaaS”

It’s:

build the AI-native operating system for tax work

Where:

  • workflows are dynamic

  • agents assist practitioners

  • data is continuously learned from

  • outcomes are automated

A simple mental model going forward

Think in layers:

  1. Data (moat)

  2. Logic / expertise (moat)

  3. Orchestration (emerging moat)

  4. UI (commodity)

Most SaaS today is stuck at layer 4.

AI flips the stack.

If you want, I can map this specifically to your KPMG environment and sketch out:

  • what gets disrupted internally

  • where to build vs buy

  • and what a “Claude Code-native tax platform” would actually look like in practice

That’s where this gets very actionable.


You said:

I’m thinking if I wanted to launch a micro startup like when Apple App Store launched and anyone could build an app and make some revenue, what would my options be now in the face of AI? This feels like a gold rush, how do I sell shovels

ChatGPT said:

You’re thinking about this the right way. But “sell shovels” in this cycle doesn’t mean building generic dev tools—those are already flooded. The real opportunity is selling leverage in a narrow, painful problem space.

The App Store analogy is useful—but incomplete. Back then:

  • constraint = distribution

  • now = attention + trust + workflow integration

So the modern “shovel” is:

something that makes AI actually useful in a real workflow, not just technically possible.

First: what not to build (the trap)

Avoid:

  • “AI wrapper” tools (chat UI + API)

  • generic agents (“AI assistant for everything”)

  • dev tools for devs (too crowded, fast-moving)

Those are the equivalent of selling generic pickaxes when factories already mass-produce them.

The real shovel categories (where money is)

These are the patterns that actually map to “micro startup with upside.”

1. Last-mile workflow tools

This is the strongest opportunity right now.

AI can do 80% of something—but the last 20% is painful.

Build:

  • tiny tools that bridge AI output → real-world action

Examples:

  • turn messy email threads into structured tasks in Teams

  • convert AI-generated docs into properly formatted, compliant deliverables

  • sync AI insights into existing systems (SharePoint, CRMs, etc.)

👉 Why this works:

  • everyone has AI

  • nobody has it properly wired into their workflow

2. Evaluation / trust layers

Right now AI outputs are… shaky.

So sell:

“confidence”

Tools like:

  • output validators

  • consistency checkers

  • “second opinion” agents

  • audit trails for AI decisions

Especially valuable in:

  • tax (your world)

  • legal

  • finance

👉 This is very underbuilt.

3. Agent infrastructure for non-technical users

People don’t want to “build agents.”

They want:

  • “when X happens, do Y reliably”

So:

  • simple agent builders

  • but opinionated and constrained

Think:

  • Zapier, but AI-native and less flexible (on purpose)

4. Data connectors (boring but gold)

This is the closest thing to selling picks and shovels.

Everyone wants AI to:

  • read their data

  • act on their systems

But integration is painful.

Build:

  • connectors to weird / legacy systems

  • normalization layers

  • clean APIs for messy sources

👉 This is unsexy and extremely monetizable.

5. Prompt / workflow packaging (verticalized)

Generic prompts are useless.

But:

  • domain-specific workflows = valuable

Example:

  • “Generate tax memo” → commodity

  • “Generate KPMG-style tax memo with correct structure, tone, and risk flags” → valuable

Sell:

  • packaged workflows

  • templates that actually work

  • tuned systems for specific jobs

6. Monitoring + reliability

AI breaks in weird ways:

  • hallucinations

  • drift

  • failures

So:

  • monitoring tools

  • alerting

  • “this output looks wrong” detection

Think:

Datadog for AI workflows

7. Human-in-the-loop systems

Pure automation is overrated.

Better model:

  • AI does draft

  • human approves edge cases

Sell:

  • approval flows

  • escalation logic

  • smart routing

What a good micro-startup looks like now

Not:

“AI app for X”

But:

“removes a specific pain in a specific workflow using AI”

Example ideas (closer to your world):

  • Email → structured engagement tracker

  • AI-generated deliverables → properly formatted client-ready docs

  • “missing work detector” (you’ve already thought about this 👀)

  • Tax position risk scanner across client portfolio

  • AI assistant that actually understands engagement context

The real “gold rush” dynamic

Here’s the honest take:

  • Building is easy → supply explodes

  • Distribution + trust = bottleneck

So your edge is:

  • domain knowledge (you have it)

  • access to real problems (you definitely have it)

  • ability to ship something small quickly (this is the lever)

A practical way to play this

If you want App Store-style upside:

Step 1: Pick a painful, repeatable task

Not broad. Something like:

  • “tracking outstanding client requests”

  • “formatting deliverables”

  • “reviewing outputs”

Step 2: Build a thin wedge tool

  • ugly UI is fine

  • solves one thing extremely well

Step 3: Charge immediately

Even:

  • $10/month

  • or per-use

You’re testing:

“is this actually valuable?”

Step 4: Expand into a workflow

Once it sticks:

  • add adjacent steps

  • become part of daily work

The subtle insight most people miss

The “shovel” isn’t AI.

AI is free enough now.

The shovel is:

making AI usable, reliable, and integrated in a real job

Where I’d push you specifically

Given your background:

You should strongly consider:

  • tax workflow augmentation

  • internal tool → external product later

  • or even just “tools for practitioners”

You’re sitting on:

  • real problems

  • real users

  • real willingness to pay (indirectly)

That’s rare.

If you want, I can give you:

  • 5–10 very specific micro-startup ideas tailored to your exact environment

  • and rank them by “fast to build vs likely to make money”

That would get you from “gold rush thinking” → “something you could ship in 2–4 weeks.”


You said:

This is outside my tax day job (unless happy accident of overlap) I think anything that would take a day of prompting that someone needs “now” could be a thing. Or with complexity to handle, like hosting. Even templates like sold on gumroad translated to AI space?

ChatGPT said:

You’re circling something real—but the “sell prompts/templates” angle alone is a weak moat. That market already behaves like stock photos: tons of supply, prices race to zero, and only a few creators win on brand.

The stronger version of your instinct is this:

Don’t sell prompts. Sell “instant, reliable outcomes” that feel like they took a day of prompting.

That’s a meaningful upgrade—and a viable micro-startup path.

The opportunity you’re sniffing out

There’s a new class of problem:

  • People know AI can do something

  • They don’t have the time / patience / skill to get it there

  • They want it done now, correctly, and formatted properly

That gap = money.

3 tiers of “AI products” (only one really works)

❌ Tier 1: Raw prompts (Gumroad-style)

  • “Best prompts for X”

  • “Ultimate ChatGPT pack”

Problem:

  • easily copied

  • low perceived value

  • one-time purchase, no retention

⚠️ Tier 2: Templates + light tooling

  • Notion templates

  • prompt + instructions

  • maybe a wrapper UI

Better, but still:

  • fragile

  • user still does work

✅ Tier 3: Outcome engines (this is the play)

You sell:

“Give me X → get polished Y in 2 minutes”

Where:

  • prompting is hidden

  • formatting is handled

  • edge cases are covered

  • output is actually usable

This is where people pay.

What makes a good “day-of-prompting product”

Your instinct is solid. The best targets are:

1. High-friction, occasional tasks

  • things people don’t do often enough to get good at

  • but when they do, they need it fast

Examples:

  • writing a business proposal

  • creating a website from scratch

  • structuring a pitch deck

  • analyzing a dataset

2. Tasks with messy inputs

  • multiple files

  • unclear instructions

  • context needed

AI alone struggles here → opportunity.

3. Tasks where formatting matters

  • PDFs

  • slides

  • structured docs

  • exports to real tools

This is huge. Most AI outputs stop short of usable.

Concrete micro-startup directions (your “shovels”)

Here are categories that map directly to what you’re thinking:

1. “One-click generators” (but actually good)

Not:

“generate a business plan”

But:

upload notes + answer 5 questions → get a polished, investor-ready doc

Key:

  • constrained input

  • strong output structure

  • opinionated

2. “AI → real-world output bridges”

Example:

  • AI generates content → your tool:

    • formats it

    • exports to Google Docs / PDF / slides

    • applies styling

This solves the “last mile” problem.

3. “Session accelerators”

You nailed this idea:

compress a full day of prompting into a guided flow

Example:

  • step-by-step wizard

  • but powered by AI behind the scenes

  • remembers context across steps

Feels like:

“expert mode AI”

4. Hosting + orchestration (lightweight)

Not full platforms—but:

  • save sessions

  • reuse workflows

  • version outputs

  • share with others

People don’t want to rebuild from scratch every time.

5. “AI kits” (this is your Gumroad 2.0 idea)

But upgraded:

Instead of:

  • prompt pack

You sell:

  • mini-app + workflow + templates + outputs

Example:

  • “Launch a micro SaaS in a weekend kit”

    • generates landing page

    • generates copy

    • deploys basic site

    • gives assets

Now it’s a tool, not content.

Business model reality check

What works now:

1. Pay-per-use

  • $5–$20 per output

  • great for occasional tasks

2. Subscription (only if repeat use)

  • if it becomes part of workflow

3. Bundle / kits

  • $20–$100 one-time

  • if high perceived value

The hidden moat (this matters)

If building is easy, what protects you?

Not:

  • code

  • prompts

But:

1. Taste

  • what “good output” looks like

  • structure, tone, completeness

2. Constraints

  • forcing users into a flow that works

3. Distribution

  • niche audience

  • specific use case

4. Iteration speed

  • quickly improving based on real usage

A sharper version of your thesis

You said:

“anything that would take a day of prompting that someone needs now”

Refine it to:

“anything where the user doesn’t want to think, iterate, or debug AI”

That’s the pain.

If I were you (practical move)

Try this:

Step 1: Pick ONE use case

Something like:

  • “turn rough idea into polished website”

  • “turn notes into presentation”

  • “analyze a CSV and produce insights”

Step 2: Build a guided flow

  • input → structured questions → output

Step 3: Obsess over output quality

  • make it feel finished

Step 4: Charge immediately

Even small.

One pushback (important)

You’re slightly over-indexing on:

“AI capability”

The real lever is:

reducing cognitive load

People don’t want power.
They want:

  • clarity

  • speed

  • confidence

If you want, I can sketch 5 very specific “day-of-prompting → product” ideas that:

  • you could build in a weekend

  • and realistically charge for

That’s the fastest way to test this instinct.


You said:

What if I create a marketplace for AI "bits" like skills... something that makes it super easy to build a killer stack with claude code, really low prices or subscription model? Maybe claude can even access it like a package library so it grabs key skill automatically, maybe we provide some review to ensure it's not harmful as part of the value add

ChatGPT said:

You’re onto something—but the raw “marketplace of AI bits” idea will collapse fast unless you sharpen where the value lives.

Right now, what you’re describing sounds like:

“npm for AI skills”

The problem is:
npm works because:

  • code is deterministic

  • interfaces are clear

  • outputs are predictable

AI “skills” are the opposite:

  • fuzzy inputs

  • inconsistent outputs

  • hard to evaluate quality

So a pure open marketplace becomes:

noisy, low-trust, race-to-zero pricing

The version that could work

The winning version is not:

a marketplace of prompts

It’s:

a trusted, composable library of outcome-driven modules

Think less “bits” and more:

mini-executables with guarantees

Reframing your idea (this is the unlock)

Instead of:

“AI skills”

Make them:

“verified capabilities”

Each one should:

  • take structured input

  • produce structured output

  • have defined behavior

  • be testable

What a “bit” should actually look like

Bad version:

  • “Write a good email”

Good version:

  • “Convert messy bullet notes into a client-ready follow-up email with clear action items and tone control”

With:

  • input schema

  • output schema

  • examples

  • edge case handling

Now it’s closer to software.

Where the real value is (your moat)

Your idea becomes interesting when you layer in:

1. Trust / verification layer

This is your strongest angle.

You mentioned:

review to ensure it's not harmful

Push that further:

  • quality scoring

  • consistency testing

  • safety checks

  • output validation

👉 You become:

“App Store review + QA for AI capabilities”

That’s valuable.

2. Composability

Not just single skills—but chains.

Example:

  • research → summarize → format → export

If users can:

  • snap these together

  • reuse flows

Now you’re not selling bits—you’re enabling systems.

3. Claude Code integration (this is key)

Your best idea in there:

Claude can pull skills automatically like a package library

If you nail this, it’s powerful.

Imagine:

  • Claude detects task

  • pulls best-rated “skill”

  • executes with known structure

That’s:

AI using tools that are standardized and trusted

4. Opinionated curation (don’t go fully open)

If you go:

  • fully open marketplace → garbage

Instead:

  • curated library first

  • then selective contributions

Think:

  • early Apple App Store, not modern chaos

Business model options (what actually works)

Option 1: Subscription for access

  • $10–$30/month

  • access to all verified skills

Works if:

  • library is high quality

  • regularly updated

Option 2: Usage-based

  • pay per execution

  • revenue share with creators

Option 3: “Pro packs”

  • bundles of workflows

  • higher price point

Option 4: Enterprise / team layer

  • private skills

  • internal sharing

  • governance

(This is where real money is later)

The biggest risk (be honest about this)

Your idea dies if:

people don’t trust that using a “skill” will give good output

So the hard problem isn’t:

  • building marketplace infra

It’s:

  • ensuring consistent, high-quality results

How to make it defensible

You need at least one of these:

1. Evaluation engine

  • automatically test skills

  • score outputs

  • detect failures

2. Standard interface

  • input/output schemas

  • predictable behavior

3. Usage data flywheel

  • which skills work best

  • continuous improvement

4. Tight integration

  • especially with tools like Claude Code

Distribution matters more than supply.

A sharper version of your idea

Here’s how I’d phrase it:

“A curated, verified capability layer that AI agents can call to reliably execute real-world tasks.”

That’s much stronger than:

“marketplace for AI bits”

If you wanted to test this fast (weekend version)

Don’t build a marketplace.

Build:

Step 1: 5–10 killer “skills”

Pick real use cases:

  • summarize + structure notes

  • generate polished docs

  • transform messy input → clean output

Step 2: Wrap them in a simple interface

  • input form

  • output preview

  • one-click run

Step 3: Add “quality guarantee”

  • examples

  • consistent formatting

  • predictable results

Step 4: Let people chain them

Even manually at first.

Final push (important)

The idea is good—but only if you avoid this trap:

thinking the value is in the supply of skills

It’s not.

The value is in:

  • trust

  • reliability

  • composability

  • distribution

If you want, I can:

  • design what a “skill spec” should look like (input/output schema, scoring, etc.)

  • and sketch what a Claude Code integration would realistically look like

That’s the difference between a neat idea and something that could actually take off.


You said:

I'm thinking someone thought "fb for agents" and built moltbook, then sold to fb for lots of $. NPM for AI prompt packages could totally be a thing, people are already peddling them on substack, but's it's not organized. Provide immediate value to reddit users, turbocharge your claude stack, people can upvote their skills etc, so we immediately start to get signal from noise. Really slick integration with command line claude code, we teach claude to access our list and grab skills that might be useful. We preload the list with known good skills. Someone needs to build the "seminars for ai agents".

ChatGPT said:

You’re not crazy—there is a real “npm-for-agents” shape emerging. But the naive version (prompt marketplace + upvotes) will drown in noise within weeks.

If you want this to be the “shovels” business, the winning version is:

a trusted runtime for agent capabilities (with a tight CLI + curation + evals),
not just a marketplace.

Where your instinct is right

  • People are already buying/selling “skills” (Substack, Gumroad).

  • Devs using Claude Code want to move faster, not handcraft prompts.

  • A CLI that can “just grab the right capability” is a killer UX.

That’s the seed of something big.

Where it breaks (if you don’t fix it)

Upvotes ≠ quality.
Reddit-style voting optimizes for novelty and vibes, not:

  • consistency

  • safety

  • correctness

Prompts aren’t modules.
Without structure, two runs give different outputs → no trust.

No standard interface.
Agents need predictable I/O. Free-form text doesn’t compose.

If you ship a pure marketplace, you’ll get:

10,000 mediocre “write better emails” skills and zero retention.

The version that can win

Reframe it as:

“npm + App Store + CI for agent capabilities”

Three pillars:

1) A strict “Skill Spec” (your API standard)

Every skill must declare:

  • Input schema (JSON)

  • Output schema (JSON)

  • Contract (what it guarantees)

  • Determinism target (how stable it is)

  • Examples / tests

This turns fuzzy prompts into callable functions.

2) Built-in evaluation (your moat)

Before a skill is “trusted,” it passes:

  • sample test suite

  • regression checks

  • format validation

  • safety checks

Then it gets a score, not just upvotes:

  • reliability (does it break?)

  • consistency (same input → similar output?)

  • quality (graded vs references)

Upvotes can exist—but as secondary signal.

3) A killer CLI integration (this is the wedge)

Think:

Bash
claude add summarize-meeting-notes
claude run summarize-meeting-notes --file notes.md

Or even:

Bash
claude auto
# Claude suggests + pulls top-rated skills for your task

Key idea:

The agent can discover, install, and call skills automatically.

This is where your “Claude grabs skills like packages” idea shines.

What users actually feel

If you nail it, the experience is:

“I don’t prompt anymore. I just use capabilities.”

That’s a big shift.

Curation strategy (don’t skip this)

Start like early Apple App Store:

  • seed with 20–50 elite skills

  • handcrafted, tested, high-quality

  • clear categories (docs, code, data, comms)

Only later:

  • open submissions

  • gated by eval + review

If you go open too early → junkyard.

What to call these (naming matters)

“Prompts” sounds cheap.

Better:

  • capabilities

  • skills (fine)

  • modules

  • actions

  • recipes

Personally: capabilities feels most “enterprise + agent-native.”

Business model (simple and viable)

Phase 1:

  • Free + open (distribution)

Phase 2:

  • Pro subscription

    • access to top-tier capabilities

    • higher eval guarantees

    • faster execution / better models

Phase 3:

  • Creator revenue share

    • paid capabilities

    • you take 20–30%

Phase 4 (real money):

  • Teams / enterprise

    • private capabilities

    • governance

    • audit logs

    • internal sharing

The real moat (not what you think)

Not:

  • the marketplace

  • the prompts

It’s:

1. Evaluation data

You’ll know which capabilities actually work.

2. Usage graph

Which skills are chained together, in what contexts.

3. Integration

If you’re embedded in dev workflows, you’re hard to replace.

“Seminars for agents” — refine this

This is actually a strong idea, just needs grounding.

Turn it into:

“Capability bundles” (guided systems, not single skills)

Examples:

  • “Launch a SaaS landing page”

  • “Analyze a dataset and produce insights”

  • “Prepare a client-ready report”

Each bundle:

  • orchestrates multiple capabilities

  • has a flow

  • produces a complete outcome

This is your premium layer.

Fastest way to test this (don’t overbuild)

Week 1:

  • Define the Skill Spec

  • Build 5–10 high-quality capabilities

Week 2:

  • Simple CLI:

    • list

    • install

    • run

Week 3:

  • Add basic scoring:

    • pass/fail tests

    • simple rating

Week 4:

  • Share with:

    • Reddit

    • dev Twitter

    • Claude Code users

Message:

“Stop prompting. Use capabilities.”

Final push (important)

You’re very close—but the difference between:

❌ “npm for prompts”
and
✅ “trusted capability layer for agents”

…is everything.

Lean into:

  • structure

  • evaluation

  • integration

Ignore:

  • generic marketplace thinking

  • social features first

If you want, I can:

  • draft a concrete Skill Spec (JSON format)

  • and design exactly how Claude Code would discover + call a capability

That would turn this from a concept into something you could actually start building this weekend.


You said:

Ok my thesis here is actually one of computational efficiency. Compute can be used to make skills with AI, but using that compute every time is wasteful, lets stand on the shoulders of giants. Much like existing libraries today to save time, but we can have people generate and consume libraries like no tomorrow, and cut down on token consumption

ChatGPT said:

Now this is a much stronger thesis—and importantly, it’s one that could actually hold up.

You’re basically saying:

“Inference is expensive. Let’s amortize it by reusing structured intelligence.”

That’s not a marketplace idea—that’s a systems optimization layer for AI.

The core insight (and why it’s legit)

Right now, most people are doing this:

User → prompt → LLM → output
(repeat endlessly)

Which is wildly inefficient because:

  • same patterns get recomputed thousands of times

  • no reuse of “reasoning work”

  • no caching beyond trivial stuff

You’re proposing:

User → capability → pre-optimized execution → output

Where:

  • the “thinking” has been partially precompiled

  • the workflow is reused

  • tokens are saved

👉 This is actually analogous to:

  • compilers vs interpreters

  • libraries vs writing code from scratch

  • caching vs recomputation

The deeper framing (this is important)

You’re not building:

“npm for prompts”

You’re building:

“a compilation + caching layer for LLM workflows”

That’s much more powerful.

Where the efficiency actually comes from

Let’s be precise, because this matters for the product.

You don’t save compute by “reusing outputs” (usually).

You save compute by:

1. Reducing iteration loops

Users normally:

  • prompt 5–20 times to get something right

A good capability:

  • gets it in 1 shot

👉 10x token reduction right there

2. Better prompt construction

A well-engineered capability:

  • uses fewer tokens

  • avoids over-explaining

  • structures context efficiently

3. Partial reuse / caching

Some parts can be reused:

  • embeddings

  • intermediate steps

  • templates

  • known transformations

4. Avoiding “exploration compute”

Most LLM usage is:

trial and error

You’re eliminating that.

Where this becomes a real business

If you lean into efficiency, your product becomes:

“Make your AI stack 5–10x cheaper and faster”

That’s a very sellable value prop.

What your system actually needs to do

To deliver on this thesis, your “skills” need to be more like:

Compiled workflows

Each one should:

  • minimize tokens

  • avoid redundant reasoning

  • structure inputs tightly

  • produce predictable outputs

This leads to a very different product

Instead of:

marketplace of skills

You build:

🧠 “Optimized capability layer for AI”

With features like:

1. Token-efficient execution

  • compressed prompts

  • minimal context

  • reusable structures

2. Deterministic-ish outputs

  • less variance

  • fewer retries

3. Composable pipelines

  • reuse outputs across steps

  • avoid recomputation

4. Caching layer

  • smart reuse of previous results

  • especially for common tasks

5. Auto-selection

  • best capability chosen for task

  • based on performance + efficiency

Your Claude Code integration becomes 🔥 here

Now your idea becomes:

Claude doesn’t just “grab a skill”
it grabs the most efficient way to solve the problem

That’s powerful.

Why this is defensible

Most people will build:

  • wrappers

  • marketplaces

  • UI tools

Very few will focus on:

  • token efficiency

  • execution optimization

  • workflow compilation

That’s deeper infrastructure.

Where this could go long-term

If you push this far enough, you get:

1. “LLM bytecode” layer

  • standardized representations of tasks

  • reusable across models

2. Cross-model optimization

  • run parts on cheaper models

  • escalate only when needed

3. Execution planning

  • like a query planner in databases

4. Cost-aware agents

  • “this task can be done 80% cheaper this way”

The subtle but critical shift

Your product is no longer:

❌ “here are cool AI skills”

It’s:

✅ “this is the cheapest, fastest way to get this done with AI”

How to test this idea (fast)

Don’t build a marketplace.

Build:

Step 1: 5 “expensive tasks”

Find things where people:

  • burn tokens

  • iterate a lot

Step 2: Optimize them brutally

  • reduce prompt size

  • reduce steps

  • improve structure

Step 3: Show comparison

Normal approach: 8 prompts, 15k tokens
Your capability: 1 prompt, 2k tokens

👉 That’s your marketing.

One pushback (important)

Be careful with this assumption:

“we can avoid recomputing everything”

LLMs don’t cache reasoning well yet.

So your real win is:

  • reducing human iteration

  • not eliminating model computation

Still very valuable—but different.

Final take

You started with:

marketplace for AI bits

You’ve now landed on:

efficiency layer for AI execution

That’s a much more interesting and durable idea.

If you want, I can:

  • design what an “optimized capability” actually looks like (prompt structure, token minimization, caching strategy)

  • and sketch how Claude Code would dynamically select the cheapest execution path

That’s where this turns into something technically differentiated, not just conceptually interesting.


You said:

Does this idea already exist?

ChatGPT said:

Short answer:
Yes — pieces of your idea already exist.
No — your full thesis (efficiency + trust + composability + agent-native runtime) does not exist yet in a clean, dominant way.

And that gap is exactly why this still feels like a gold rush.

🧭 What already exists (and how close it is)

1. Prompt marketplaces (your “Gumroad layer”)

  • PromptBase has hundreds of thousands of prompts for sale PromptBase

  • Other clones exist (Prompt marketplaces, GitHub repos, etc.) GitHub+1

👉 Reality:

  • Supply is huge

  • Quality is inconsistent

  • Little reuse beyond copy/paste

Verdict: closest to your idea superficially, but not defensible

2. “AI skill” / agent marketplaces (closer)

  • Systems like OpenClaw have thousands of installable “skills” TechRadar

  • There are already “agent marketplaces” and experiments with reusable components Reddit

👉 Reality:

  • This is basically your idea… but:

    • messy

    • unsafe (literally malware issues) The Verge

    • not standardized

    • not optimized for efficiency

Verdict: closest structurally, but broken in execution

3. Claude Code / component libraries (your wedge already forming)

  • There are sites with 1000+ prebuilt Claude Code components, agents, and skills Claude Code Templates

👉 Reality:

  • People are already:

    • sharing reusable “skills”

    • building stacks

  • But:

    • no strong curation

    • no evaluation

    • no cost optimization

Verdict: your idea is already trying to happen organically

4. “AI skills = new npm” narrative (you’re not alone)

There’s active thinking that:

AI skills are reusable packets of reasoning, like npm packages Dev Digest

👉 Reality:

  • The idea is trending

  • But infrastructure is immature

Verdict: you’re aligned with where smart people are heading

5. Adjacent but different: model marketplaces

  • OpenRouter routes prompts to the cheapest/best model automatically The Wall Street Journal

👉 Reality:

  • They optimize model selection

  • You’re proposing optimizing workflow execution

Verdict: complementary, not competitive

🧠 What does NOT exist yet (this is your opening)

No one has successfully combined:

1. Reusable capabilities

2. Strong evaluation / trust layer

3. Agent-native integration (auto-discovery + execution)

4. Compute / token efficiency as first-class goal

Most current systems fail on:

AreaCurrent State
Qualityinconsistent
Safetyrisky
Interfacesunstructured
Reuseshallow
Efficiencynot optimized
Trustlow

⚠️ The biggest signal from the market

The fact that:

  • OpenClaw skills have malware issues The Verge

  • marketplaces are chaotic

  • people are rebuilding the same stuff

…is actually bullish for your idea.

It means:

demand exists, but no one has solved trust + structure yet

🔥 Where your idea is genuinely new

Your unique angle is:

Compute efficiency as the organizing principle

Nobody is really saying:

“Let’s reduce token burn by standardizing reusable reasoning”

Most are saying:

  • “let’s share prompts”

  • “let’s build agents”

  • “let’s sell skills”

You’re saying:

“let’s eliminate redundant thinking”

That’s deeper.

🧭 So where do you sit?

You are building something like:

LayerExisting?You
Prompt marketplace✅ saturated❌ don’t go here
Skill marketplace⚠️ early + messy⚠️ partial
Agent platforms⚠️ fragmented⚠️ adjacent
Optimized capability layer❌ not solved✅ your space

💡 Brutally honest conclusion

  • The idea category exists

  • The winning implementation does not

This is exactly like:

  • early app stores (before Apple cleaned it up)

  • early npm (before standards)

  • early AWS (before abstraction)

🚀 The real opportunity (your positioning)

Don’t say:

“marketplace for AI skills”

Say:

“The most efficient way to execute tasks with AI”

or

“Reusable, optimized capabilities for agents”

🧠 Final thought (important)

If you build this as:

  • marketplace → you compete with PromptBase (bad)

If you build this as:

  • infrastructure → you’re early

If you want, I can:

  • map the current players into a competitive landscape

  • and show exactly where a wedge product could win (and not get crushed)

That’s the difference between a cool idea and a real shot at something big.


You said:

Help me think through the architecture, and how I could scale over night. Can the skills be hosted on various githubs to save me server costs, just links? We're just the aggregator with metadata? We build the CLI component, is it just an edit to claude.md to check the skill index on the regular? How do we provide access to cc to access our skill index? Can anthropic hard code avoiding my skill index? If someone uses the skill, can we log that and uprank the skill in our index?

ChatGPT said:

I’m checking what Claude Code actually supports today so I can anchor this in something buildable rather than hand-wavy. Then I’ll map an architecture that can stay cheap and still scale.

I found a cleaner wedge than editing CLAUDE.md: Claude Code now has first-class plugins, skills, MCP, and even custom plugin marketplaces. That means your MVP can ride the official extension path instead of fighting it.

Yes. And the good news is the cleanest version is more official than you were assuming.

Claude Code already supports first-class skills, plugins, custom plugin marketplaces, MCP servers, and hooks. Skills are reusable SKILL.md-based capabilities that Claude can invoke automatically or by slash command; plugins are the packaging layer for distributing skills/hooks/MCP; and marketplaces are catalogs that can point to plugins hosted in GitHub or other git repos. Claude Code also lets a repo pre-register additional marketplaces via settings. Claude+3Claude API Docs+3Claude+3

So I would not start by editing CLAUDE.md to poll your index. CLAUDE.md is just persistent project/user instructions loaded into sessions; it is not the real extension/distribution surface. Anthropic’s own docs push multi-step reusable procedures into skills, and team/community distribution into plugins and marketplaces. Claude API Docs+2Claude API Docs+2

The architecture I’d use

Think in four layers.

1. The registry

Your product is mainly a registry + ranking + trust layer.

Keep a central index that stores:

  • plugin id

  • skill names and descriptions

  • source repo URL

  • version / tag / commit pin

  • categories

  • safety / quality metadata

  • popularity stats

  • verified/unverified badge

This can be extremely cheap at first: a git repo containing marketplace.json plus your own extra metadata files, served from GitHub. Claude Code marketplaces already support GitHub and generic git sources, including pinning by branch, tag, or commit. Claude+1

2. The package hosting layer

Yes, you can keep costs low by having the actual plugins/skills live on various GitHub repos and let your marketplace point to them.

That is very aligned with the official model: a marketplace file lists plugins and where to find them, and those plugin sources can be GitHub repos, git URLs, subdirectories, and more. Claude+1

I would still enforce one rule:

  • your registry should store a pinned commit SHA for every approved release

That gives you reproducibility and prevents a maintainer from swapping in malicious content after approval.

3. The Claude Code integration layer

Your wedge is a plugin marketplace + helper plugin + optional CLI.

You have three integration options:

Best option: run as a proper Claude Code marketplace.
Users add your marketplace with Claude Code’s plugin marketplace flow, then install plugins from it. Repos can also pre-register a marketplace via extraKnownMarketplaces. Claude+1

Second option: ship a helper plugin.
That plugin can include:

  • a discovery skill

  • an MCP server for search/recommendation

  • hooks for light telemetry or prompting

  • maybe an agent specialized in “find the right capability”

Plugins can bundle skills, agents, hooks, and MCP servers together. Claude+1

Third option: an external CLI.
Useful for:

  • browsing/searching your registry

  • installing plugins

  • pinning versions

  • verifying signatures

  • reporting opt-in usage

But I would not make this the only path, because the official plugin/marketplace path is stronger.

4. The intelligence layer

This is where your product gets defensible.

Build:

  • search and recommendation

  • quality scoring

  • safety review

  • version trust

  • usage-based ranking

  • “people who installed X also use Y”

That is your moat, not storage.

How I’d make it “scale overnight”

You do not need a heavy backend to survive launch day.

Cheap-first design

Use:

  • GitHub repo for marketplace/index

  • Cloudflare in front if you want caching and a custom domain

  • optional tiny API for search/analytics

  • Postgres/Supabase only when you need richer ranking

This way most read traffic hits static content. Plugin code is fetched from the source repos, not your servers.

Scale points

Your likely bottlenecks are not CPU. They are:

  • search latency

  • ranking freshness

  • analytics writes

  • moderation workflow

So keep the initial hot path mostly static:

  1. user adds marketplace

  2. Claude Code fetches marketplace metadata

  3. user installs plugin from source repo

  4. local Claude Code auto-discovers skills from the installed plugin

Plugins and skills are automatically discovered when installed, and Claude can invoke them automatically based on context. Claude

That means you can have a lot of growth before you need serious infrastructure.

Should skills be “just links” and you’re the aggregator?

Mostly yes.

That’s probably the right first move.

Your registry can be:

  • metadata

  • validation

  • ranking

  • discovery

  • trust badges

And the packages themselves can stay distributed in maintainer-owned repos.

That saves hosting cost and liability, and makes onboarding creators easier.

I would add two things:

  • mirror critical metadata in your own index

  • pin every approved version to a commit SHA

Otherwise you are trusting mutable upstreams too much.

Should the CLI just edit CLAUDE.md?

No.

That’s the wrong abstraction.

Use:

  • plugin marketplace

  • plugin install

  • repo/user settings

  • optional MCP tool

CLAUDE.md is useful for project instructions and reminders, but it is not the best mechanism for package discovery/distribution. Anthropic explicitly separates CLAUDE.md from skills and plugins. Claude API Docs+2Claude API Docs+2

A better CLI would do things like:

  • skills search pdf

  • skills install vendor/pdf-toolkit

  • skills verify vendor/pdf-toolkit

  • skills trust marketplace yourmarket

  • skills stats vendor/pdf-toolkit

Under the hood it can:

  • add your marketplace if missing

  • install/update plugins

  • optionally configure a helper MCP server

How do you provide access to Claude Code to access your skill index?

Two strong paths.

Path A: marketplace-native

Users add your marketplace, then Claude Code knows about plugins from that marketplace. Repos can also suggest or pre-register additional marketplaces using extraKnownMarketplaces. Claude+1

Path B: MCP-native

You run an MCP server that exposes tools like:

  • search_capabilities

  • recommend_capabilities

  • install_capability

  • rate_capability

  • get_trust_report

Claude Code supports MCP servers as official tool integrations. Anthropic also warns that MCP servers are user-trusted integrations, not something Anthropic audits for you. Claude API Docs+1

My take:

  • marketplace for distribution

  • MCP for discovery/search/recommendation

That combo is strongest.

Can Anthropic hard-code avoiding your skill index?

They could change product behavior at any time. I would not build assuming privileged or permanent placement.

But today, Claude Code explicitly supports:

  • custom plugins

  • custom marketplaces

  • repo-level marketplace registration

  • MCP integrations

  • hooks

  • local/user/project settings

So there is a supported way for users and teams to adopt your ecosystem without Anthropic “blessing” it. Claude API Docs+3Claude+3Claude+3

The risk is less “Anthropic blocks me tomorrow” and more:

  • they launch their own better marketplace

  • they privilege their own official marketplace in UX

  • enterprises restrict external marketplaces through policy settings

Docs show managed settings can enforce policies around marketplaces and MCP, so enterprise environments may lock this down. Claude+1

So the strategic answer is:

  • do not rely on a loophole

  • rely on official extension points

  • build enough value that users actively choose your registry

Can you log skill usage and uprank skills?

Yes, but not automatically from “a link exists on GitHub.”

If someone installs and runs a plugin locally, your registry will not magically know unless you add an observation path.

You have a few options:

Best option: opt-in telemetry through your helper plugin

Because plugins can include hooks, and hooks can fire on events like SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, PreToolUse, and PostToolUse, you can have your plugin report anonymized usage events to your service. Claude+1

Example:

  • user invokes /yourmarket:find-pdf-skill

  • helper plugin installs or recommends vendor/pdf-toolkit

  • hook reports “recommended”, “installed”, “invoked”, maybe “completed successfully”

MCP option

If usage flows through your MCP discovery/install tools, you can log those server-side.

CLI option

If people install via your CLI, your CLI can report installs and updates.

Ranking

Then uprank with weighted signals:

  • installs

  • active usage

  • repeat usage

  • completion rate

  • manual rating

  • maintainer reputation

  • verified badge

  • freshness

  • safety incidents

Do not use raw upvotes alone. They are gameable.

What I would actually build first

Phase 1: zero-server-ish MVP

  • one GitHub marketplace repo

  • 20–50 curated plugins

  • pinned versions

  • a tiny website for browsing

  • optional CLI for install/search

  • no telemetry yet, maybe just stars/download-like signals

Phase 2: helper plugin + MCP

  • helper plugin that can search your registry

  • MCP server for recommendations and install suggestions

  • trust badges and human review

  • basic anonymous telemetry

Phase 3: ranking and safety moat

  • verified builds

  • static analysis on plugin contents

  • commit pinning

  • maintainer trust score

  • sandboxed testing

  • quality score, not just votes

My blunt recommendation

Your first product should not be “the marketplace.”

It should be:

the best curated Claude Code capability registry, with one-click install and trust signals

And technically that means:

  • official plugin marketplace underneath

  • GitHub-hosted plugin sources

  • helper plugin and/or MCP for discovery

  • optional CLI for ergonomics

  • telemetry only with explicit opt-in

That gives you the cheapest path, the most compatibility with Claude Code as it exists today, and the best chance of turning “link farm” into something valuable.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete MVP spec with:

  • repo structure

  • marketplace JSON shape

  • plugin layout

  • MCP endpoints

  • and a ranking formula.


You said:

Do we need to be installing these, or can claude go look at a skill in real time and leverage it during it's processing?

ChatGPT said:

I’m checking the exact behavior here, because the distinction between “installed locally” and “fetched on demand” matters a lot for your architecture.

Mostly no: Claude Code does not normally go fetch an arbitrary remote skill file in real time and use it ad hoc. The supported model is that skills/plugins are installed locally first, then Claude can discover and invoke them. Anthropic’s docs say marketplaces are for discovering and installing plugins, and once a marketplace plugin is fetched it is copied into the local plugin cache under ~/.claude/plugins/cache. Skills themselves live in local locations like project .claude/skills, user ~/.claude/skills, or inside installed plugins. Claude+2Claude+2

So architecturally, you should assume:

Baseline path

  • your index helps Claude/user find a capability

  • the capability/plugin gets installed locally

  • then Claude can use it during processing

That is the clean, official path today. Plugins are the package unit; they can bundle skills, hooks, agents, and MCP servers. Claude+1

There is one important nuance: Claude can use remote things in real time through MCP servers or HTTP/shell hooks, because those are live tool integrations. But that is different from “Claude sees a GitHub skill and just reads it on the fly.” MCP is an explicit connected tool surface, not a remote skill file loader. Claude+2Claude+2

So I’d think of the choices like this:

Option 1: installable skills/plugins

  • best for reusable packaged capabilities

  • lowest ambiguity

  • works with marketplaces

  • good for version pinning and trust

Option 2: live MCP-backed registry

  • your server exposes search_skill, recommend_skill, maybe run_skill

  • Claude queries that server in-session

  • better for discovery and dynamic recommendations

  • but then you are operating more infrastructure

That leads to a strong hybrid architecture:

Best hybrid

  • Registry/index stays remote

  • Packaged skills/plugins get installed locally

  • Optional MCP server handles search/recommendation/analytics

That way:

  • cheap hosting

  • local execution for most capabilities

  • real-time intelligence only where it adds value

If your goal is ultra-low friction, you could make the MCP server return:

  • “best matching skills”

  • install command

  • trust score

  • compatibility info

Then the user or helper plugin installs the selected capability.

The one thing I would not bet on is:

“Claude will periodically browse my remote GitHub skill library during normal thinking and just absorb skills without install.”

That does not appear to be the official model today. Claude API Docs+2Claude+2

My practical recommendation:

  • MVP: local install required

  • Delight layer: MCP search/recommendation so it feels dynamic

  • Later: helper plugin that makes install nearly invisible

That gives you the UX you want without depending on unsupported behavior.


You said:

That's too bad. Just to check, if we install a plugin that I make, could that plugin automatically intercept prompts/claude's thinking and fetch relavent skills?

ChatGPT said:

I’m checking the official Claude Code hook/plugin behavior, because the key question is whether a plugin can transparently inject capability discovery into the loop without doing anything sketchy.

Yes — a plugin you ship can intercept the user prompt before Claude processes it and do work at that point. Claude Code plugins support the same lifecycle hook events as user-defined hooks, including UserPromptSubmit, which fires “when you submit a prompt, before Claude processes it.” Claude+1

That means your plugin could plausibly do things like:

  • inspect the incoming prompt,

  • call your own API or MCP server to look for relevant skills,

  • inject extra context or recommendations,

  • or block / redirect certain flows. The hooks docs explicitly say UserPromptSubmit can be used to “add additional context based on the prompt/conversation, validate prompts, or block certain types of prompts.” Claude+1

But there are two important limits:

1. It is not literally intercepting Claude’s hidden reasoning

You can hook the submitted prompt and lifecycle events, not Claude’s private chain-of-thought. The supported events are around prompt submission, tool use, notifications, session start/end, and similar execution points. Claude+2Claude+2

2. “Fetch relevant skills” is doable, but using them may still depend on the integration path

Your plugin can definitely search a remote index in real time from a hook, because hooks can run commands, make HTTP requests, and integrate external systems. Claude API Docs+1

The question is what happens next:

  • If your hook returns extra context / instructions / recommendations, Claude can use that immediately. Claude+1

  • If you want Claude to gain a new packaged skill in the official sense, that still points toward installation/local availability, because installed plugins are what automatically expose skills to Claude. The plugins reference says skills are automatically discovered when the plugin is installed. Claude

So the cleanest model is:

Hook/plugin intercepts prompt → queries your registry → injects recommended instructions or offers install → Claude proceeds.

That gives you the “dynamic fetch” feeling without needing Anthropic to support arbitrary remote skill loading.

Best practical design

I would structure it like this:

Helper plugin

  • owns a UserPromptSubmit hook

  • sends prompt metadata to your registry/MCP

  • gets back:

    • relevant skill recommendations

    • maybe a small trusted instruction block

    • maybe an install suggestion

Your registry

  • ranks skills

  • returns top candidates

  • can include trust score and tags

Optional install path

  • if a skill is worth persisting, install the actual plugin/package locally afterward

So yes, you can get surprisingly close to what you want:

  • dynamic discovery at prompt time: yes

  • hooking into the user’s turn before Claude processes it: yes

  • reading/modifying hidden chain-of-thought: no

  • magically treating arbitrary remote skill files as first-class installed skills without install: not the official path today. Claude+1

The most interesting wedge is probably:

a small always-installed “broker” plugin that dynamically consults your index on each prompt.

That’s much stronger than asking users to install dozens of separate skills up front.


You said:

I like that... why don't we build this for me, and then see if it's actually useful

ChatGPT said:

I’m going to put together a concrete starter kit: a broker plugin, a tiny registry service, and a README so you can actually try this rather than just talk about it.

I’ve got enough of the current Claude Code shape to make a realistic starter kit. I’m building it around the official plugin + hook + marketplace path, with a tiny broker service behind it.

I’ve built the starter kit. I’m just verifying the local test path now so I can tell you exactly what is real versus what still needs adjustment.

I built you a working starter kit.

Files:

What’s inside:

  • a broker plugin with a UserPromptSubmit hook

  • a tiny registry service that ranks matching skills

  • a sample marketplace.json

  • a sample skill index

  • a test payload so you can simulate prompt interception locally

Why this shape:

  • Claude Code supports plugins that can include hooks and skills, and UserPromptSubmit fires before Claude processes the prompt. Skills are auto-discovered once a plugin is installed. Claude+1

  • Claude Code also supports custom plugin marketplaces via .claude-plugin/marketplace.json, and those plugins can be sourced from relative paths, GitHub repos, git URLs, or npm. Installed plugins are copied into the local plugin cache. Claude+1

The architecture I encoded is:

user prompt → broker hook → remote registry search → compact additionalContext injection → Claude proceeds

That gives you the “dynamic fetch” feeling without relying on unsupported remote skill loading. The marketplace/plugin path remains the durable install path. Claude+2Claude+2

A couple of honest caveats:

  • I verified the Python hook and registry flow locally.

  • I did not validate the plugin against a real Claude Code install in this environment, so you should still run claude plugin validate broker-plugin on your machine before using it. The docs expose that validation flow explicitly. Claude

The most important files to open first are:

  • README.md

  • broker-plugin/.claude-plugin/plugin.json

  • broker-plugin/hooks/prompt_broker.py

  • registry-service/app.py

My next recommendation would be to make the broker smarter in one narrow way:

  • add a small install helper so when the broker recommends a skill, Claude can suggest the exact install command and track whether the user actually adopted it.

If you want, I’ll turn this into a cleaner v2 with:

  • SHA-pinned marketplace entries

  • opt-in usage logging

  • a better ranking formula

  • and a second sample skill package so you can test the full broker → install → invoke loop end to end.


You said:

Can you walk me through setup like a dummy sorry

ChatGPT said:

I’m going to make this very step-by-step and grounded in the current Claude Code docs so you can follow it without guessing.

Absolutely. Here’s the dumbed-down version.

What you have

Inside the zip, there are two main things:

1. The registry service
This is a tiny local web server.
Its job is: when Claude sees your prompt, it asks this server, “do you know any relevant skills for this?”

2. The broker plugin
This is the piece that plugs into Claude Code.
Its job is: intercept your prompt before Claude handles it, ask the registry for relevant skills, and pass a little extra context back to Claude. Claude Code supports plugins with hooks, and UserPromptSubmit runs before Claude processes your prompt. Claude+2Claude+2

So the flow is:

you type prompt → broker plugin sees it → broker asks local registry → registry returns suggested skills → Claude gets extra context

What you need on your computer

You need:

  • Claude Code installed

  • Python 3 installed

  • the zip file extracted somewhere easy, like your Desktop

Claude Code plugins are a real supported feature, and plugins can contain hooks and skills. Claude+1

Step 1: unzip the folder

Unzip skill-broker-mvp.zip.

You should end up with a folder called something like:

skill-broker-mvp

Inside it you’ll see:

  • broker-plugin/

  • registry-service/

  • README.md

Step 2: start the registry service

Open Terminal.

Go into the registry service folder. For example:

Bash
cd ~/Desktop/skill-broker-mvp/registry-service

If your folder is somewhere else, adjust the path.

Now run:

Bash
python3 app.py

If it starts properly, it should stay running and listen locally.

This is your little local “skill index brain.”

Leave this terminal window open.

Step 3: test the registry by itself

Open another Terminal window.

Go to the same folder:

Bash
cd ~/Desktop/skill-broker-mvp/registry-service

Then try:

Bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8000/search \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"prompt":"help me summarize meeting notes into action items"}'

You should get JSON back with suggested skills.

If that works, your registry is alive.

Step 4: validate the broker plugin

Open another Terminal window and go to the project root:

Bash
cd ~/Desktop/skill-broker-mvp

Then run:

Bash
claude plugin validate broker-plugin

Claude Code has a real plugin validation flow, and recent versions improved claude plugin validate to catch schema/frontmatter issues. Claude+1

If it says valid, good.

If it errors, copy the error and send it to me.

Step 5: install the broker plugin

Still in the skill-broker-mvp folder, run:

Bash
claude plugin install ./broker-plugin

That should install your plugin locally.

Claude Code plugins are installed locally, and installed plugins are the normal path for adding hooks/skills/components. Skills are then auto-discovered from installed locations. Claude+1

Step 6: confirm Claude Code sees the plugin

Try:

Bash
claude plugin list

You want to see your broker plugin in the list.

If that works, the plugin is installed.

Step 7: try Claude Code with a prompt

Now go into any test folder or repo and start Claude Code like you normally would.

Then give it a prompt like:

Help me turn these rough notes into a polished project summary and action list.

Because the plugin hook runs on UserPromptSubmit, it should inspect that prompt before Claude processes it and ask your registry for a matching skill suggestion. Hooks can add extra context at prompt time. Claude+1

What you’re looking for:

  • Claude seems to “know” about the recommended skill/context

  • no hook errors

  • no timeout

Step 8: if it does not work, test the hook directly

Inside the project there should be a sample payload or test file.

You can often test the hook script directly with something like:

Bash
cd ~/Desktop/skill-broker-mvp/broker-plugin/hooks
python3 prompt_broker.py < ../../test-payload.json

That lets you see:

  • whether the hook script itself works

  • whether it can talk to the registry

  • what JSON it returns

If this step works but Claude Code does not, the problem is plugin wiring.
If this step fails, the problem is the Python script or registry.

What success looks like

A good first success is not “magic.”

A good first success is:

  • registry runs

  • plugin validates

  • plugin installs

  • hook fires

  • registry returns relevant skill suggestions

  • Claude gets a small context boost

That proves the architecture.

The most likely problems

“claude: command not found”

Claude Code is not installed, or not in your PATH. Anthropic’s troubleshooting docs explicitly mention checking for multiple or conflicting installations with commands like which -a claude. Claude API Docs

Python errors

Usually means:

  • wrong folder

  • wrong Python version

  • missing dependency

Plugin validate fails

Usually means:

  • plugin schema mismatch

  • wrong file path

  • malformed JSON/YAML

Hook runs but no skill suggestions

Usually means:

  • registry is not running

  • broker is pointing at wrong port

  • your prompt does not match any sample skill in the index

The simplest exact order to try

Do these in order:

Bash
cd ~/Desktop/skill-broker-mvp/registry-service
python3 app.py

Open new Terminal:

Bash
cd ~/Desktop/skill-broker-mvp
claude plugin validate broker-plugin
claude plugin install ./broker-plugin
claude plugin list

Then open Claude Code and try a relevant prompt.

What not to worry about yet

Do not worry yet about:

  • marketplaces

  • GitHub hosting

  • analytics

  • ranking

  • auto-installs

  • scaling

For now, all we are proving is:

can a Claude Code plugin catch a prompt and fetch useful skill suggestions from a registry?

That’s the only test that matters first.

If you want, next message I can walk you through this as a literal live checklist: “type this now… what do you see?”


You said:

It didn't seem to start. no error, just new command line

ChatGPT said:

I’m checking the exact server file so I can tell you what “normal” looks like and where it may be silently exiting.

That explains it.

app.py does not start a server by itself. It only defines the FastAPI app. So when you run:

Bash
python3 app.py

Python loads the file and exits cleanly, which is why you just got a new prompt.

Use this instead:

Bash
cd ~/Desktop/skill-broker-mvp/registry-service
python3 -m uvicorn app:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000 --reload

If uvicorn is not installed, first run:

Bash
python3 -m pip install fastapi uvicorn

Then start it again with:

Bash
python3 -m uvicorn app:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8000 --reload

What you should see:

  • lines mentioning Uvicorn

  • something like “running on http://127.0.0.1:8000”

  • the terminal stays busy instead of returning to a prompt

Then in a second Terminal window, test it:

Bash
curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/health

You should get:

JSON
{"status":"ok"}

Then test search:

Bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8000/search \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"summarize meeting notes","top_k":3}'

One correction to my earlier instructions: the request field is query, not prompt.

So use:

JSON
{"query":"..."}

not

JSON
{"prompt":"..."}

After that, move on to plugin install.

Run these next:

Bash
cd ~/Desktop/skill-broker-mvp
claude plugin validate broker-plugin
claude plugin install ./broker-plugin
claude plugin list

If you paste me the exact output from the uvicorn command, I’ll walk you to the next step.


You said:

Can I paste this and run all at once, or paste line by line?

ChatGPT said:

Good question—this one’s a bit subtle.

Short answer:

You should paste it all at once as a single command.

Why

Those backslashes \ mean:

“this command continues on the next line”

So this:

Bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8000/search \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"summarize meeting notes","top_k":3}'

is actually one command split across multiple lines for readability.

Two ways to run it

✅ Option 1 (recommended): paste all at once

Just copy the whole block and paste it into Terminal → hit Enter.

✅ Option 2: single line version

If you prefer, you can run it like this:

Bash
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8000/search -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"query":"summarize meeting notes","top_k":3}'

❌ What NOT to do

Don’t paste line-by-line like this:

curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8000/search
-H "Content-Type: application/json"
-d '{"query":"..."}'

That will break, because each line becomes a separate command.

Quick sanity check

After you run it, you should see JSON come back.

If you get:

  • nothing → server not running

  • connection refused → wrong port / server not started

  • error → paste it here and I’ll debug with you

Next step after this works: we wire it into Claude 👍


You said:

Invoke-WebRequest : A parameter cannot be found that matches parameter name 'X'. At line:1 char:6 + curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8000/search \ + ~~ + CategoryInfo : InvalidArgument: (:) [Invoke-WebRequest], ParameterBindingException + FullyQualifiedErrorId : NamedParameterNotFound,Microsoft.PowerShell.Commands.InvokeWebRequestCommand

ChatGPT said:

Ahh—this tells me you’re on Windows PowerShell, not bash. 👍

In PowerShell, curl is actually an alias for Invoke-WebRequest, so the -X, -H, -d flags don’t work the same way.

✅ Use this instead in PowerShell

Run this:

PowerShell
Invoke-RestMethod `
  -Uri "http://127.0.0.1:8000/search" `
  -Method POST `
  -ContentType "application/json" `
  -Body '{"query":"summarize meeting notes","top_k":3}'

What you should see

You should get back something like:

JSON
{
  "results": [...]
}

If that still feels annoying

You have 2 easier options:

Option A: Use a browser

Just go to:

http://127.0.0.1:8000/docs

FastAPI gives you a UI where you can:

  • click /search

  • click “Try it out”

  • paste:

JSON
{
  "query": "summarize meeting notes",
  "top_k": 3
}

  • hit Execute

👉 This is honestly the easiest way.

Option B: Use Git Bash (if installed)

If you have Git installed, open Git Bash and your original curl command will work exactly as written.

Quick checkpoint

Before moving on, confirm:

  • your server is still running (uvicorn window open)

  • the /search endpoint returns results

Once that works, we’ll hook Claude into it (this is where it gets interesting).


You said:

results ------- {@{id=meeting-notes-polisher; name=Meeting Notes Polisher; summary=Convert rough meeting notes into clean summaries ...

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — that means the registry is working.

You’ve proven:

  • the server is running

  • /search works

  • your sample skill index is returning matches

Now we move to Claude plugin setup.

Next step

Open a new PowerShell window and go to the project folder:

PowerShell
cd "C:\path\to\skill-broker-mvp"

Replace that with the real path.

Then run:

PowerShell
claude plugin validate broker-plugin

After that, run:

PowerShell
claude plugin install .\broker-plugin

Then:

PowerShell
claude plugin list

What I want you to tell me

Paste back the output from:

  • claude plugin validate broker-plugin

  • and if that works, claude plugin install .\broker-plugin

If claude is not recognized

Run:

PowerShell
claude --version

If that fails, Claude Code either is not installed or is not on your PATH.

Tiny note

Keep the registry server window open while doing this.
That first PowerShell window running uvicorn needs to stay alive.

Run the validate command next and paste me exactly what it says.


You said:

✘ Failed to install plugin ".\broker-plugin": Plugin ".\broker-plugin" not found in any configured marketplace

ChatGPT said:

That error is actually helpful — it means Claude interpreted your path like a marketplace package name instead of a local plugin source. I’m checking the exact local install syntax so I can give you the right command instead of guessing.

Yep — that’s the issue.

You tried to install a local plugin directly, but Claude was expecting a plugin from a configured marketplace. The docs show two different dev paths:

  • for local plugin testing, run Claude with --plugin-dir ./my-plugin — that loads the plugin directly without installation Claude

  • for marketplace installs, first add a marketplace, then install plugin-name@marketplace-name Claude+1

Fastest way right now

From your project folder, do this:

PowerShell
claude --plugin-dir .\broker-plugin

That should start Claude Code with your plugin loaded for that session. This is the official local development/testing flow. Claude

Then inside Claude Code:

  • try your normal prompt

  • if you edit the plugin, run /reload-plugins to pick up changes without restarting Claude

If you want it “installed” properly

Then you need a local marketplace first.

From the docs, the flow is:

PowerShell
claude plugin marketplace add .\some-marketplace-folder

or inside Claude Code:

/plugin marketplace add ./path/to/marketplace

Then install with:

/plugin install plugin-name@marketplace-name

Claude supports adding a marketplace from a local directory containing .claude-plugin/marketplace.json. Claude+1

What I recommend

For now, do not fight marketplace install.

Just run:

PowerShell
claude --plugin-dir .\broker-plugin

That is the cleanest way to test whether the broker is useful at all. Claude

If you want, next I’ll walk you through exactly what you should see after launching with --plugin-dir, and how to confirm the hook is actually firing.


You said:

Ok done this, claude loaded, how do we test?

ChatGPT said:

Nice—this is the fun part. Let’s verify the broker is actually doing something.

🧪 What we’re testing

We want to prove:

Your plugin sees the prompt → calls your registry → injects extra context → Claude uses it

Step 1: Confirm the registry is still running

You should still have that first terminal open with something like:

Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000

If not, restart it.

Step 2: Add a visible debug signal (VERY helpful)

Right now the hook is probably silent.

Let’s make it obvious it’s firing.

Open this file:

broker-plugin/hooks/prompt_broker.py

Find where it returns the result (something like a JSON response).

Add this line inside the returned context:

Python
"additionalContext": f"[BROKER HIT] Suggested skill: {skill_name}\n\n{existing_context}"

Or even simpler:

Python
"additionalContext": "[BROKER HIT]\n\n" + existing_context

Save the file.

Step 3: Reload the plugin

Inside Claude Code, run:

/reload-plugins

Claude Code supports reloading plugins without restarting. (code.claude.com)

Step 4: Try a targeted prompt

Use something that should clearly match your test skill:

I have messy meeting notes. Turn them into a clean summary with action items.

Step 5: What you’re looking for

If it’s working, you should see:

  • Claude’s response includes something like:

    [BROKER HIT]

    OR

  • Claude clearly follows the style of your “Meeting Notes Polisher” skill

Step 6: If nothing happens

We debug in this order:

Check A: Did the hook fire?

Add this at the top of prompt_broker.py:

Python
print("BROKER HOOK FIRED")

Then:

  • run /reload-plugins

  • send another prompt

👉 Look at the Claude terminal output
You should see:

BROKER HOOK FIRED

If not → hook isn’t wired correctly.

Check B: Did it hit the registry?

Add:

Python
print("Sending request to registry...")

and:

Python
print("Registry response:", response.text)

If you don’t see this → your hook isn’t calling the registry.

Check C: Did the registry respond?

You should see logs in the uvicorn window when Claude sends a prompt.

If not → wrong URL or port in your hook.

Step 7: If it works (this is the key moment)

If you see [BROKER HIT] show up in Claude’s response…

👉 You just proved:

  • Claude can be intercepted

  • external intelligence can be injected

  • your registry can influence behavior in real time

That’s the core of your product.

What success really means here

Not perfection.

Just:

  • hook fires

  • registry returns something

  • Claude gets nudged

Even a dumb suggestion proves the architecture.

Next step (after this works)

Then we make it actually useful:

  • better matching (keywords → embeddings later)

  • smarter context injection

  • recommend installable skills

  • track usage

Do this now

  1. Add the [BROKER HIT] marker

  2. Reload plugins

  3. Run the meeting notes prompt

Then tell me:

  • Do you see the marker?

  • Do you see any logs in the terminal?

We’ll tighten it from there.


You said:

Where exactly do i put this

ChatGPT said:

Got it — let’s do this very precisely so you don’t have to guess.

🎯 What we’re modifying

File:

broker-plugin/hooks/prompt_broker.py

We’re going to modify the return value of the hook.

🔍 Step 1: Find the return block

Open prompt_broker.py and look for something like this near the bottom:

Python
return {
    "additionalContext": existing_context
}

OR it might look like:

Python
return {
    "additionalContext": context
}

OR even:

Python
return {
    "additionalContext": ""
}

✏️ Step 2: Modify that line

Replace the value with this:

Python
return {
    "additionalContext": "[BROKER HIT]\n\n" + existing_context
}

❗ If existing_context does NOT exist

If you don’t see a variable named existing_context, just do this instead:

Python
return {
    "additionalContext": "[BROKER HIT]\n\n"
}

That’s totally fine for testing.

✅ Example (complete simple version)

Your function might end like this:

Python
def handle_prompt(...):
    # your logic above

    return {
        "additionalContext": "[BROKER HIT]\n\n"
    }

💡 Optional (better debug)

Also add this at the very top of the file:

Python
print("BROKER HOOK FIRED")

So we can confirm it’s even running.

🔄 Step 3: Reload plugin

Inside Claude Code:

/reload-plugins

🧪 Step 4: Test

Run:

Turn these rough meeting notes into a clean summary and action list

🎯 What you should see

One of these:

Best case

Claude response starts with:

[BROKER HIT]

Or

You see in the terminal:

BROKER HOOK FIRED

If you’re unsure

If you want, paste your entire prompt_broker.py here and I’ll point to the exact line for you.


You said:

Somewhere in here? def main() -> int: payload = _read_stdin_json() prompt = payload.get("prompt", "") if not isinstance(prompt, str) or not prompt.strip(): print(json.dumps({"continue": True})) return 0 results = _search(prompt) _log(prompt, results) additional_context = _build_context(results) output: Dict[str, Any] = {"continue": True} if additional_context: output["additionalContext"] = additional_context print(json.dumps(output)) return 0 if __name__ == "__main__": raise SystemExit(main())

ChatGPT said:

Yes — exactly there.

Change this part:

Python
output: Dict[str, Any] = {"continue": True}
if additional_context:
    output["additionalContext"] = additional_context

to this:

Python
output: Dict[str, Any] = {"continue": True}
if additional_context:
    output["additionalContext"] = "[BROKER HIT]\n\n" + additional_context
else:
    output["additionalContext"] = "[BROKER HIT]\n\n"

So your full main() should look like:

Python
def main() -> int:
    payload = _read_stdin_json()
    prompt = payload.get("prompt", "")

    if not isinstance(prompt, str) or not prompt.strip():
        print(json.dumps({"continue": True}))
        return 0

    results = _search(prompt)
    _log(prompt, results)
    additional_context = _build_context(results)

    output: Dict[str, Any] = {"continue": True}
    if additional_context:
        output["additionalContext"] = "[BROKER HIT]\n\n" + additional_context
    else:
        output["additionalContext"] = "[BROKER HIT]\n\n"

    print(json.dumps(output))
    return 0

Then do this:

  1. Save the file.

  2. In Claude Code, run:

/reload-plugins

  1. Then try:

I have messy meeting notes. Turn them into a clean summary with action items.

If it works, Claude should now get that injected marker.

If you want an even stronger debug signal, also add this near the top of main() after prompt = ...:

Python
print("BROKER HOOK FIRED", file=sys.stderr)

If you do that, make sure sys is imported at the top:

Python
import sys

Then you can see whether the hook ran at all.


You said:

Output: ● Share the notes and I'll clean them up.

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — this tells us something very important:

👉 Your hook is NOT being used yet

Because if it was, you would see [BROKER HIT] in the response.

So now we debug wiring, not logic.

🔍 Step 1: Did the hook run at all?

Add this line near the top of main():

Python
import sys
print("BROKER HOOK FIRED", file=sys.stderr)

So it looks like:

Python
def main() -> int:
    payload = _read_stdin_json()
    prompt = payload.get("prompt", "")

    import sys
    print("BROKER HOOK FIRED", file=sys.stderr)

🔄 Step 2: Reload plugin

Inside Claude:

/reload-plugins

🧪 Step 3: Try ANY prompt

Example:

hello

👀 What you should check

Look at the terminal where Claude is running

Do you see:

BROKER HOOK FIRED

🎯 Interpretation

Case 1: You DO see it

👉 Hook is working
👉 Problem is with context injection (easy fix)

Case 2: You DO NOT see it

👉 Hook is NOT wired correctly
👉 This is the most likely case

🚨 If hook is NOT firing (most likely)

We need to confirm the plugin is actually registering the hook.

Open this file:

broker-plugin/.claude-plugin/plugin.json

Look for something like:

JSON
"hooks": {
  "UserPromptSubmit": {
    "command": "python3 hooks/prompt_broker.py"
  }
}

❗ On Windows this is often the issue

python3 may not exist.

Replace it with:

JSON
"hooks": {
  "UserPromptSubmit": {
    "command": "python hooks/prompt_broker.py"
  }
}

🔄 Then:

  1. Save plugin.json

  2. Run in Claude:

/reload-plugins

  1. Try again

🧠 Why this is happening

Right now Claude is just behaving normally:

“Share the notes and I’ll clean them up”

That means:

  • no hook ran

  • no registry call happened

  • no context injection occurred

🧪 Goal for next message

Tell me:

👉 Do you see "BROKER HOOK FIRED" in the terminal?

That answer determines the next step immediately.

We’re very close — this is just one wiring issue.


You said:

I feel it might be too fast to see coming from claude output? Can we monitor somewhere else?

ChatGPT said:

Yes — easiest is to log to a file instead of stderr.

Do this in prompt_broker.py.

At the top, add:

Python
from pathlib import Path

Then inside main(), right after:

Python
prompt = payload.get("prompt", "")

add this:

Python
log_file = Path(__file__).parent / "broker_debug.log"
with log_file.open("a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    f.write("HOOK FIRED\n")
    f.write(f"PROMPT: {prompt}\n\n")

So that section becomes:

Python
def main() -> int:
    payload = _read_stdin_json()
    prompt = payload.get("prompt", "")

    log_file = Path(__file__).parent / "broker_debug.log"
    with log_file.open("a", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write("HOOK FIRED\n")
        f.write(f"PROMPT: {prompt}\n\n")

    if not isinstance(prompt, str) or not prompt.strip():
        print(json.dumps({"continue": True}))
        return 0

Then save the file and run in Claude:

/reload-plugins

Then send any prompt.

After that, check this file:

broker-plugin/hooks/broker_debug.log

If the file appears and has new lines in it, the hook is firing.
